exemplar.title: How to determine the orbital speed of an exoplanet using the Doppler effect

detailedExemplarData.title: How to determine the orbital speed of an exoplanet using the Doppler effect

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Topic is communicated adequately with clear purpose and adequate explanation.
Research question is explicitly stated and connected to the discussion.
Methodology is mostly complete with relevant sources and a reproducible procedure.
weaknesses: Research question combines measurement and evaluation, reducing focus.
Some linguistic imprecision and minor errors detract from clarity.
Methodological justification (e.g., scaling factor, calibration) is underdeveloped.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work sits in the 3â4 band for Focus and Method. The title and opening clearly introduce the topic, and the RQ drives the investigation, but ambiguity between âdeterminingâ and âevaluatingâ orbital speed prevents a fully focused question. The procedure lists materials and steps in a reproducible manner, yet key justifications (choice of scaling factor, control of variables) are weak. Overall, the student demonstrates adequate clarity and an essentially complete methodology, meriting 4/6.

strengths: Correct derivation and application of Doppler and orbitalâspeed equations.
Use of relevant astrophysics concepts and source material.
Terminology is generally accurate and concepts are employed coherently.
weaknesses: Misapplication of soundâwave scaling to light without justification.
Occasional spelling/capitalization errors (e.g., âdoppler,â âradical velocityâ).
Unit inconsistencies between m/s and km/s.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates good subject knowledge and mostly accurate use of terminology, placing the work in the 3â4 band. Core derivations are correct and physics principles are applied, but occasional conceptual slips (mixing classical and relativistic formulations, unit inconsistencies) prevent an upperâband score. A score of 4/6 reflects solid understanding with room for greater precision and consistency.

strengths: Analysis remains focused on the research question, showing clear relevance.
Argument follows a coherent structure, linking theory, results, and evaluation.
Evaluation identifies key limitations and suggests plausible improvements.
weaknesses: Data analysis is simplistic (single trial per frequency, unvalidated scale factor).
Argument occasionally relies on circular reasoning using selfâderived theoretical benchmarks.
Evaluation is descriptive rather than quantitatively critical (impact of errors not fully assessed).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although individual strands were assessed at the lower end of the 4â6 band, Critical Thinking carries double weighting. The studentâs analysis is consistently relevant, the argument is clear and logically ordered, and the evaluation goes beyond superficial comment. To reflect the relative importance of this criterion and consistent midâupper performance across its strands, an overall score of 8/12 is awarded, placing the work in the lower 7â9 band of âgoodâ critical thinking.

strengths: Clear overall structure with title page, table of contents, headings, and bibliography.
Equations and figures are integrated into the text to support explanations.
weaknesses: Some figures are low resolution or unreferenced in the text.
Inconsistent heading levels, missing page numbers in the TOC, and minor typographical errors.
Redundancies and layout inconsistencies create occasional large blank spaces.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets EE conventions with a clear structure and readability, situating it in the 3â4 band. However, unresolved layout inconsistencies (missing page numbers, unreferenced or lowâquality figures, typos) prevent it from reaching the highest band. A score of 3/4 reflects effective communication tempered by these presentation flaws.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key Physics terms (radial velocity, red/blue shift, orbital speed) are used largely correctly, yet there are recurrent spelling and capitalization errors (e.g., âdoppler,â âradical velocityâ) and occasional imprecise symbols. Concepts are conveyed adequately but not with consistent accuracy or sophistication, aligning with the 3-4 band.

A logical sequence from derivation to results to evaluation is present, yet argumentation is occasionally circular (using own calculated âtheoreticalâ value as benchmark) and reasoning gaps appear (e.g., choice of 10 000 factor). Structure is clear but not robustly justified; exposition remains adequate rather than well-developed, suiting a score of 4 within the 4-6 range.

The student outlines a reproducible school-laboratory method (rotating ruler, phone, microphone, Logger Pro) and lists materials, but justification of choices (e.g., scaling factor 10 000, selected frequencies, single rotation speed) is weak. Relevant sources are cited, yet there is limited evidence of critically informed selection or completeness (e.g., no calibration, no control of temperature). This reflects a mostly complete but not fully appropriate methodology, fitting band 3-4 at the lower end.

The essay includes correct derivations of the Doppler and orbitalâspeed equations and uses basic astrophysics principles correctly. Nevertheless, misconceptions ariseâsound-wave results are linearly scaled to light without physical justification, and âcentrifugal forceâ wording shows partial misunderstanding. Source material is applied but not always critically. This demonstrates good but not excellent knowledge, meriting the middle band at 3.

The student identifies several limitationsâinstrument precision, mechanical instability, scaling assumptionsâand suggests future improvements. Evaluation is mostly descriptive and does not quantify impacts or propose alternative models, but it goes beyond superficial mention. Hence the work fits solidly within âgood evaluation with some critical analysisâ, warranting a mid-band score of 4.

The title, abstract-style opening and repeated statements of purpose make the topic understandable and linked to Physics. However, phrasing such as âHow to determineâ¦â and âHow effective isâ¦â reveals some ambiguity and overlap between aim and evaluation, and minor linguistic errors detract from precision. Overall clarity is adequate but not consistently concise or sharply delimited, matching the middle bandâs expectation for sufficient but not fully refined communication.

Figures are inserted and labelled, but some are low-resolution or unreferenced; mathematical formatting drifts, and large blank spaces emerge between sections. The layout supports comprehension but with notable inconsistencies, matching the 1-2 band at the higher point of 2.

The research question is explicitly stated, yet it merges efficiency (âhow effectiveâ) with measurement (âdetermine orbital speedâ), resulting in partial focus. It is nonetheless connected to the ensuing discussion of Dopplerâbased speed determination. Because the focus is present but only moderate and could be tighter, a score toward the lower end of the 3-4 band is appropriate.

Data are collected for five emitted frequencies and analysed to obtain velocity, including uncertainty via standard deviation. However, analysis is simplistic (single trial per frequency, sign ignored, arbitrary averaging, unvalidated scale factor). Relevance to the RQ is evident but depth is limited, matching the lower end of the 4-6 band.

The essay follows EE conventions with title page, table of contents, headings, equations and bibliography. Minor issues (duplicate plus signs, inconsistent heading levels) are present but do not obstruct reading. Thus the structure is clear and broadly appropriate, meriting the upper band with a score of 3.

annotations.comment: The title communicates the topic well but could be more precise; here 'How to determineâ¦' conjoins aim and evaluation. Consider rephrasing to focus on a specific aspect, e.g. 'To what extent can Doppler shifts quantify exoplanet orbital speed?'
Word count is provided and within limit, satisfying the formatting requirement.
The table of contents lists sections but omits corresponding page numbers, hindering navigation. Include page numbers to improve structure and usability.
The 'Table of Contents' heading is formatted consistently, but ensure heading hierarchy matches conventions (e.g., use H1 or H2 style).
The introductory anecdote engages but remains overly personal; strengthen the academic focus by linking this narrative directly to the research question and physics concepts.
The research question merges effectiveness and measurement, making it broad. Narrow the focus by specifying the experimental parameters or expected outcome.
A formal hypothesis is missing; define a precise, testable statement to anchor methodology and analysis.

Background description of HD 209458 b lacks specific citation details and contains spelling inconsistency (âradical velocityâ instead of âradial velocityâ). Correct terminology and ensure precise citations.
Figure 1 caption is vague ('u.d.'); include full source details and clarify what 'u.d.' denotes to improve referencing.
The term 'doppler effect' should be consistently capitalized as 'Doppler effect' to reflect proper terminology.
This paragraph outlines experimental aims well but could benefit from clearer explanation of how the scaling factor is justified; currently the rationale for factor 10000 is deferred.
Sentence fragment 'Factors such as noise interferenceâ¦These limitations are' is incomplete. Ensure completeness of sentences and continuity in description of limitations.
The theoretical description of the Doppler Effect mixes sound and light without clear distinction; consider separating classical and relativistic formulations to avoid confusion.
The sentence ends abruptly; ensure full sentences in explanations to maintain clarity in theoretical development.
Equation (1) is correctly presented but missing explanation of the sign convention; include description of when to use plus or minus to clarify usage.
The derivation to isolate v_s omits step explaining why observer speed v_0 is set to zero; explicitly state this assumption to justify the simplification.
The cross-multiplication in equation (3) is accurate but would benefit from a transition sentence explaining each algebraic step for reader clarity.
Final derived equation (8) correctly expresses v_s, yet the origin of Îf notation is sudden; define Îf earlier or insert a reference to its derivation.
Description of redshift vs blueshift contains a typo ('took' instead of 'tool'); proofread for technical accuracy and terminology.
Ensure figure captions are consistently formatted; include figure number and source in the same style as earlier references.
Link the definition of centripetal and gravitational forces more explicitly; here the connection is stated but transition could be smoother to set up equation (9).
Newtonâs law of gravitation (9) is correctly used; consider adding units to G directly next to the equation for immediate reference.
Final orbital speed formula (13) is accurate; however, remind reader of its dependence on circular orbit assumption to contextualize its application.
When explaining the cancellation of m, refer explicitly to the physical significance (mass cancels for orbiting speed independence), deepening conceptual understanding.
Equation (14) repeats result from prior page; avoid redundancy or merge similar equations to streamline presentation.
Footnote citation formatting is inconsistent; ensure all citations follow a single style and include complete access dates.
Conversion from AU to meters uses correct values but shows multiple stellar mass values in text; choose a single authoritative source for consistency.
Mass conversion equation is clear; however, specify the number of significant figures justified by the precision of the input to avoid overprecision.
The square root calculation yields 382.7 km/s, but unit km/s conflicts with earlier m/s conventions; align units throughout to maintain consistency.
This paragraph anticipates evaluation but repeats earlier rationale; condense to avoid redundancy and focus on linking theoretical result to experimental design.
Materials list includes rubber bands and drill, but lacks calibration equipment (e.g., tachometer); discuss how source speed was measured or controlled.
Picture 3 shows setup clearly; include a scale bar or labels indicating key distances to improve reproducibility.
Procedure describes frequency intervals but omits number of trials per frequency; specify trial count to ensure method replicability.
Step 2 mentions ruler attachment but doesnât state radius of rotation; include numerical value for r to support subsequent calculations.
